caption: Fig. 1 Typical optical micrographies of the alloys composed of, (a) 5 mass%Mn, (b) 10 mass%Mn, (c) 15 mass%Mn, (d) 20 mass%Mn, (e) 25 mass%Mn, (f) 30 mass%Mn, (g) 35 mass%Mn and (h) 40 mass% Mn, respectively.
paper_abstract: The microstructures and mechanical properties of the Fe–10 mass%Al–(5–40)mass%Mn–1.0 mass%C alloys sheet castings have been investigated by using optical microscope (OM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), scanning electron microscope (SEM), experimental model analysis, tensile test and hardness test. Based on the present studies, the macroscopic microstructure of the present alloys are a mixture of austenite (γ ) and ferrite (α) duplex phases, and the α phase would decrease with the Mn content increased. In the meantime, according to the examination of TEM, the macroscopic γ phase is a mixture of the γ + fine(Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ) + (Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ ) phases and the macroscopic α phase is a mixture of the D03 + coarse(Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ ) phases. The mechanical properties such as the ultimate tensile strength (UTS), yield strength (Y.S.), elongation of these alloys were in the range of 646–887 MPa, 575–824 MPa, and 16.3–29.2%, respectively. The hardness of these alloys were in the range of HRC 31–44. Moreover, the relationship between hardness and ultimate strength is UTS 20.93HRC. In addition, the damping ratios and Young’s modulus of the present alloys were in the range of 0.0603–0.0417, and 139–165 GPa, respectively. It is noted here that the results have never observed by other research workers in the Fe–Al–Mn–C alloy system.
paper_title: Effects of Mn Contents on the Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of the Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C Alloy
citation_sentences: The typical OMs of the present alloys are shown in Fig. 1(a) through 1(h), revealing the dendritic solidification occurred. Macroscopically, there is a mixture region of α phase (white region) and $\gamma$ phase. The α phase consisted in the alloys decreases with increasing Mn content, and the alloy with 40 mass%Mn content is essentially a single $\gamma$ phase. Based on the image software analyzing, the fraction of the α phase using ten specimens for average value of each alloy was examined, as shown in the Table 2. Neglected the effects of the aluminum and carbon contents, it could be also found that the ratios between the percentage of the α phase and Mn mass% contents for various alloys is $\alpha \% =$ $0 . 0 2 4 4 ( \mathrm { M n } \% ) ^ { 2 } - 2 . 7 4 4 ( \mathrm { M n } \% ) + 6 8 . 6 5$ with the error of about 0.93%.

A typical TEM examination is described as following. Figure 2 shows a bright field (BF) electron micrograph of the Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C alloy (alloy C). It is obvious that it consists of two regions as marked A and B, where the $\mathbf { \ddot { A } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ region is an α phase and the $\mathbf { \ddot { \delta } } \mathbf { \vec { B } } ^ { \prime }$ region is a γ phase by comparing with the Fig. 1(c). Figure 3(a) shows a BF electron micrograph taken from the B region in Fig. 2, revealing that some precipitates marked as C and extremely fine precipitates marked as D were formed within the $\gamma$ matrix. Figures 3(b) and (c), two [001] selected-area diffraction patterns (SADP) taken from the $\gamma$ matrix for different size of precipitates, reveal that in addition to the reflection spots of the $\gamma$ phase, the SADPs also consist of small superlattice spots. Since the diffraction patterns for the $\mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } 1 _ { 2 }$ structure phase and the L12 structure phase are very similar, it is necessary to compare the intensities of diffraction spots from each phase. The structure factor of the $\mathrm { L ^ { \prime } } 1 _ { 2 } \mathrm { - p h a s e }$ precipitate in $\mathrm { F e - A l - M n - C }$ alloy system can be written as $F _ { h k l } = f _ { \mathrm { A l } } + f _ { \mathrm { F e / M n } } \times \{ \cos ( h + k ) \pi +$ cos $( k + l ) \pi + \cos ( l + h ) \pi \} + f _ { \mathrm { c } } \times \cos ( h + k + l ) \pi$ , where $f _ { \mathrm { A l } }$ is the atomic scattering amplitude for Al atoms, $f _ { \mathrm c }$ is that for C atoms and $f _ { \mathrm { F e / M n } }$ is that for Fe or Mn atoms corrected by their fraction. Comparing to the $\mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } 1 _ { 2 }$ -structure phase, for the randomly distributed carbon atom within the structure, the structure factor of the L12-phase precipitate is simply given by $F _ { h k l } = f _ { \mathrm { A l } } + f _ { \mathrm { F e / M n } } \times \{ \cos ( h + k ) \pi + \cos ( k + l ) \pi +$ cos $( l + h ) \pi \} = f _ { \mathrm { A l } } \pm f _ { \mathrm { F e / M n } }$ . By taking the chemical composition of the present study into account, the structure factor ratio for (100) and (110) reflections of the $_ { \mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } 1 _ { 2 } - \mathrm { s t r u c t u r e } }$ is near 9.1 and of L12-structure being near 1.1. For the intensity of diffraction spot approximating to the square of the structure factor, the intensity of (100) spot for $\mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } 1 _ { 2 }$ structure phase is much stronger than that of (110) reflection. And the intensity for (100) and (110) reflections of the $\operatorname { L } 1 _ { 2 } .$ -structure is similar. Therefore, from these superlattice spots and the fact that superlattice 100 spots are much stronger than the 110 spot, it is deduced that the precipitates are the $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ carbides having an order $\bar { \mathrm { L ^ { \prime } } } 1 _ { 2 } \mathrm { - t y p e ~ s t r u c t u r e } ^ { 8 - 1 6 , 2 4 , 3 0 - 3 9 ) }$ with the lattice parameter $a = 0 . 3 8 3 \mathrm { - } 0 . 3 8 6 \mathrm { n m }$ . The lattice parameter of $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ carbides is larger than that reported by other worker.9, 15, 29–31) In the Figs. 3(b) and (c), it is also found that satellites could be observed along

caption: Fig. 2 A bright field electron micrograph taken from the Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C alloy.
paper_abstract: The microstructures and mechanical properties of the Fe–10 mass%Al–(5–40)mass%Mn–1.0 mass%C alloys sheet castings have been investigated by using optical microscope (OM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), scanning electron microscope (SEM), experimental model analysis, tensile test and hardness test. Based on the present studies, the macroscopic microstructure of the present alloys are a mixture of austenite (γ ) and ferrite (α) duplex phases, and the α phase would decrease with the Mn content increased. In the meantime, according to the examination of TEM, the macroscopic γ phase is a mixture of the γ + fine(Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ) + (Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ ) phases and the macroscopic α phase is a mixture of the D03 + coarse(Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ ) phases. The mechanical properties such as the ultimate tensile strength (UTS), yield strength (Y.S.), elongation of these alloys were in the range of 646–887 MPa, 575–824 MPa, and 16.3–29.2%, respectively. The hardness of these alloys were in the range of HRC 31–44. Moreover, the relationship between hardness and ultimate strength is UTS 20.93HRC. In addition, the damping ratios and Young’s modulus of the present alloys were in the range of 0.0603–0.0417, and 139–165 GPa, respectively. It is noted here that the results have never observed by other research workers in the Fe–Al–Mn–C alloy system.
paper_title: Effects of Mn Contents on the Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of the Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C Alloy
citation_sentences: A typical TEM examination is described as following. Figure 2 shows a bright field (BF) electron micrograph of the Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C alloy (alloy C). It is obvious that it consists of two regions as marked A and B, where the $\mathbf { \ddot { A } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ region is an α phase and the $\mathbf { \ddot { \delta } } \mathbf { \vec { B } } ^ { \prime }$ region is a γ phase by comparing with the Fig. 1(c). Figure 3(a) shows a BF electron micrograph taken from the B region in Fig. 2, revealing that some precipitates marked as C and extremely fine precipitates marked as D were formed within the $\gamma$ matrix. Figures 3(b) and (c), two [001] selected-area diffraction patterns (SADP) taken from the $\gamma$ matrix for different size of precipitates, reveal that in addition to the reflection spots of the $\gamma$ phase, the SADPs also consist of small superlattice spots. Since the diffraction patterns for the $\mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } 1 _ { 2 }$ structure phase and the L12 structure phase are very similar, it is necessary to compare the intensities of diffraction spots from each phase. The structure factor of the $\mathrm { L ^ { \prime } } 1 _ { 2 } \mathrm { - p h a s e }$ precipitate in $\mathrm { F e - A l - M n - C }$ alloy system can be written as $F _ { h k l } = f _ { \mathrm { A l } } + f _ { \mathrm { F e / M n } } \times \{ \cos ( h + k ) \pi +$ cos $( k + l ) \pi + \cos ( l + h ) \pi \} + f _ { \mathrm { c } } \times \cos ( h + k + l ) \pi$ , where $f _ { \mathrm { A l } }$ is the atomic scattering amplitude for Al atoms, $f _ { \mathrm c }$ is that for C atoms and $f _ { \mathrm { F e / M n } }$ is that for Fe or Mn atoms corrected by their fraction. Comparing to the $\mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } 1 _ { 2 }$ -structure phase, for the randomly distributed carbon atom within the structure, the structure factor of the L12-phase precipitate is simply given by $F _ { h k l } = f _ { \mathrm { A l } } + f _ { \mathrm { F e / M n } } \times \{ \cos ( h + k ) \pi + \cos ( k + l ) \pi +$ cos $( l + h ) \pi \} = f _ { \mathrm { A l } } \pm f _ { \mathrm { F e / M n } }$ . By taking the chemical composition of the present study into account, the structure factor ratio for (100) and (110) reflections of the $_ { \mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } 1 _ { 2 } - \mathrm { s t r u c t u r e } }$ is near 9.1 and of L12-structure being near 1.1. For the intensity of diffraction spot approximating to the square of the structure factor, the intensity of (100) spot for $\mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } 1 _ { 2 }$ structure phase is much stronger than that of (110) reflection. And the intensity for (100) and (110) reflections of the $\operatorname { L } 1 _ { 2 } .$ -structure is similar. Therefore, from these superlattice spots and the fact that superlattice 100 spots are much stronger than the 110 spot, it is deduced that the precipitates are the $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ carbides having an order $\bar { \mathrm { L ^ { \prime } } } 1 _ { 2 } \mathrm { - t y p e ~ s t r u c t u r e } ^ { 8 - 1 6 , 2 4 , 3 0 - 3 9 ) }$ with the lattice parameter $a = 0 . 3 8 3 \mathrm { - } 0 . 3 8 6 \mathrm { n m }$ . The lattice parameter of $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ carbides is larger than that reported by other worker.9, 15, 29–31) In the Figs. 3(b) and (c), it is also found that satellites could be observed along

reciprocal lattice direction around the (200) and (220) reflections spots. The existence of the satellites suggests that the $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ carbides were formed within the austenite matrix during cooling by spinodal decomposition or the chemical fluctuations were occurred between the $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ carbides and the austenite matrix. For convenience, the $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ carbides and κ carbides were used to represent the particle $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ precipitates and extremely fine $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ precipitates. Shown in Fig. 3(d) is a 100 $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ dark-field (DF) electron micrograph, exhibiting the presence of $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ carbides and both the $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ carbides and κ carbides have the same orientation. Similarly, based on the TEM examination, the macroscopic austenite region of the $\mathrm { F e - 1 0 A l - } x \mathbf { M } \mathbf { n } { - } 1 . 0 \mathbf { C }$ alloys, alloy A ∼ alloy H, contains some $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ and κ phase precipitates. Figure 4 shows a SADP taken from the coarse particle marked as $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime }$ in Fig. 2, it is found that the particle is also a $( \mathrm { F e , M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C , }$ x (represented as $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime } )$ carbide, same as observed in the matrix. However, from the same strength of superlattice 100, 120 and 110 spots, it is deduced that the particle is having an ordered $_ \mathrm { L 1 } _ { 2 } \mathrm { - t y p e }$ structure with the lattice parameter $a = 0 . 3 8 7 – 0 . 3 8 9 \mathrm { n m }$ . It also means that the $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime }$ carbide having $\mathrm { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ structure may be formed at a high temperature resulted in more random, disorder carbon distribution.3, 13, 16) Figures 5(a) and (b) are two SADPs taken from the matrix marked as A in Fig. 2, revealing that in addition to the reflection spots of the α phase, the SADPs also consist of some superlattice spots. From these superlattice spots, it is deduced that the α matrix has an order $\mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 ^ { - } }$ type structure with the lattice parameter $a = 0 . 5 8 6 { - 0 . 5 8 8 \mathrm { n m } }$ . Figure 6 is a SADP taken from the D03 matrix and the particle $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime } .$ , indicating that the orientation relationship between the $\mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 }$ matrix and the $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime }$ phase is $[ 1 \bar { 1 } 1 ] _ { \kappa } ~ \parallel ~ [ 2 2 0 ] _ { \mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 } }$ and $( 0 1 1 ) _ { \kappa } ~ \parallel ( 0 0 1 ) _ { \mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 } }$ . Figures 7(a) and (b), two 111 and 200 $\mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 }$ DF electron micrographs, clearly exhibit the presences of a/4(200) and $a / 4 ( 1 1 1 )$ anti-phase boundaries (APDs), respectively. From the fact that the $a / 4 ( 1 1 1 )$ APDs are small than the a/4(200) APDs, it indicates that the crystal structure of the matrix at high temperatures should be a disordered

α phase and the $\mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 }$ domains were formed during quenching through a $\alpha \mathrm { B } 2 \mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 }$ continuous ordering transition. Similarly, based on the TEM examination, the α region of the $\mathrm { F e - 1 0 A l - } x \mathrm { M n - 1 }$ .0C alloys is all the $\mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 }$ phase containing some coarse $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime }$ phase precipitates. To sum up, the microstructure of the various Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C sheet castings is a mixture of $( \gamma + \kappa + \kappa ^ { \prime } )$ region and $( \mathrm Ḋ 0 Ḍ _ { 3 } + \kappa ^ { \prime \prime } )$ region and only the alloy with 40% Mn content is a single γ phase. Moreover, based on TEM examination, it would be observed the amount of $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime }$ precipitates along the $\gamma / \alpha$ boundaries, as shown in Fig. 2, are higher than within the $\mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 }$ matrix. However, any β-Mn phase could not be observed in the present study. It is noted here that the results have never observed by other research workers in the Fe–Al–Mn–C alloy system.

caption: Fig. 7 (a) $g = 1 1 1$ dark field, and (b) $g = 2 0 0$ dark field electron micrograph taken with the D03 phase.
paper_abstract: The microstructures and mechanical properties of the Fe–10 mass%Al–(5–40)mass%Mn–1.0 mass%C alloys sheet castings have been investigated by using optical microscope (OM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), scanning electron microscope (SEM), experimental model analysis, tensile test and hardness test. Based on the present studies, the macroscopic microstructure of the present alloys are a mixture of austenite (γ ) and ferrite (α) duplex phases, and the α phase would decrease with the Mn content increased. In the meantime, according to the examination of TEM, the macroscopic γ phase is a mixture of the γ + fine(Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ) + (Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ ) phases and the macroscopic α phase is a mixture of the D03 + coarse(Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ ) phases. The mechanical properties such as the ultimate tensile strength (UTS), yield strength (Y.S.), elongation of these alloys were in the range of 646–887 MPa, 575–824 MPa, and 16.3–29.2%, respectively. The hardness of these alloys were in the range of HRC 31–44. Moreover, the relationship between hardness and ultimate strength is UTS 20.93HRC. In addition, the damping ratios and Young’s modulus of the present alloys were in the range of 0.0603–0.0417, and 139–165 GPa, respectively. It is noted here that the results have never observed by other research workers in the Fe–Al–Mn–C alloy system.
paper_title: Effects of Mn Contents on the Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of the Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C Alloy

For convenience, the $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ carbides and κ carbides were used to represent the particle $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ precipitates and extremely fine $( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { x }$ precipitates. Shown in Fig. 3(d) is a 100 $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ dark-field (DF) electron micrograph, exhibiting the presence of $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ carbides and both the $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ carbides and κ carbides have the same orientation. Similarly, based on the TEM examination, the macroscopic austenite region of the $\mathrm { F e - 1 0 A l - } x \mathbf { M } \mathbf { n } { - } 1 . 0 \mathbf { C }$ alloys, alloy A ∼ alloy H, contains some $\kappa ^ { \prime }$ and κ phase precipitates. Figure 4 shows a SADP taken from the coarse particle marked as $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime }$ in Fig. 2, it is found that the particle is also a $( \mathrm { F e , M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C , }$ x (represented as $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime } )$ carbide, same as observed in the matrix. However, from the same strength of superlattice 100, 120 and 110 spots, it is deduced that the particle is having an ordered $_ \mathrm { L 1 } _ { 2 } \mathrm { - t y p e }$ structure with the lattice parameter $a = 0 . 3 8 7 – 0 . 3 8 9 \mathrm { n m }$ . citation_sentences: Tensile properties are shown in Table 3. When the Mn was added, the UTS and Y.S. of the present alloys significantly increased. With the 15 mass%Mn content, the alloy sheet casting possesses maximum UTS = 887 MPa and $\mathrm { Y . S . } = 8 2 4 \mathrm { M P a }$ . However, the UTS and Y.S. gradually decreased as the Mn content more than 20 mass%, as shown in Fig. 8(a). In Table 3 or Fig. 8(b), it is also found that the elongation of the present alloy are in the range of 16.3–29.2%. In addition, based on the hardness test shown in Table 3, it is found that the hardness of the present alloy are in the range of $\mathrm { H R _ { C } } 3 1 \mathrm { - } 4 4$ , and the relationship between the strength and hardness is nearly $\mathrm { U T S } ~ \equiv ~ 2 0 . 9 3 \mathrm { H R _ { C } }$ with the error about 2.1%. Figures 9(a) through (c) are three SEM electron micrographs showing the fracture surfaces of specimens taken from the Fe–10Al–10Mn–1.0C alloy (alloy B), the Fe–10Al– 25Mn–1.0C alloy (alloy E) and the Fe–10Al–35Mn–1.0C alloy (alloy G), respectively. Compared with the microstructures of the alloys, it implies that the $( \mathrm { D } 0 _ { 3 } + \kappa ^ { \prime \prime } )$ two-phase region would play an important role to the fracture mechanism.

citation_sentences: Figures 10(a) through (h) are the frequency response function of the present alloys. The natural frequencies of the Fe– 10Al–xMn–1.0C alloy containing Mn of 5.12, 9.85, 14.89, 20.06, 24.87, 30.45, 34.88, 39.73 mass%, with various thickness are 226, 238, 232, 226, 236, 234, 242 and 242 Hz, respectively. The damping ratios are in the range between 0.06 and 0.04 as shown in Fig. 11, which are extracted through the FRF by the half-power point method. In addition, it is found that damping ratios of the present alloys are decreased as the Mn content increased. Model analysis of the cantilever beam was also performed via finite element analysis to fit the natural frequencies by adjustment of Young’s modulus. Based
